narrative: I'm sorry to share this news, but your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 59.6% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is just over the threshold we use to flag higher-risk applicants. Your profile includes being in the bottom decile for grades in both your first and third years of school, an undergraduate GPA of 2.30, and an LSAT score of 29.50— all notably lower than the averages of 3.21 for GPA, 36.70 for LSAT, and around the middle deciles for school rankings. You're from an upper-middle income family, identify as male and Asian, and plan to attend full-time, which aligns closely with the majority of students in our dataset. The model weighed these details to forecast your bar exam performance, and several stood out as the strongest drivers behind its concern. Most influential was your LSAT score, which had the biggest push toward predicting failure compared to typical stronger scorers. Right behind it was your third-year school decile ranking at the bottom, signaling early struggles that carried weight. Your first-year decile, also at the bottom, ranked next in influence, reinforcing a pattern of lower academic standing relative to peers. Your family income bracket came fourth, nudging the prediction further toward risk despite being upper-middle. Your racial background as Asian followed, then your undergraduate GPA, with gender and full-time status having the least sway—all of these top factors contributed toward the failure prediction rather than success. In contrast to typical students who pass the bar, who tend to have mid-to-high deciles, LSATs above 36, and GPAs over 3.2, your metrics fell short in the areas that mattered most here. To improve your chances next time, focus on bolstering your academic record—consider retaking the LSAT to aim higher, strengthening any future coursework or grad-level grades to climb those decile rankings, and building study habits proven to boost bar passage. With targeted effort in these changeable areas, you could shift the model's view and open doors to admission. Keep pushing; many have turned similar profiles around with persistence.
